Name: rate_distortion_model_compression
Title: Towards Achieving Theoretical Limits in Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Framework
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified rate-distortion framework can provide tight upper and lower bounds on the achievable compression rates for neural networks, leading to systematic pruning and quantization strategies that adhere to information-theoretic principles.
Related Work: Prior works, such as those by Isik et al. and Zheng et al., have explored the application of rate-distortion theory to model pruning and quantization. However, these works primarily focus on empirical results without deriving a comprehensive theoretical framework that consolidates various compression techniques under a single rate-distortion paradigm. Our proposal distinguishes itself by aiming to derive and rigorously demonstrate achievable rate-distortion bounds for different compression strategies, providing a theoretical foundation that can guide practical implementations.
Abstract: In this research proposal, we aim to bridge the gap between theoretical limits and practical applications in model compression through a comprehensive rate-distortion framework. By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we will derive achievable compression bounds that can inform pruning and quantization strategies for neural networks. Our approach will quantify the trade-off between compression rates and model accuracy, establishing a solid theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of compression techniques. We will explore the interaction between model architecture and information-theoretic measures, such as mutual information and entropy, to develop a systematic methodology for optimizing model compression. Through rigorous mathematical analysis, we aim to provide insights that not only enhance existing methods but also lay the groundwork for future advancements in efficient model deployment across various applications.
Experiments: 1. Derive theoretical bounds on compression rates for various neural network architectures using rate-distortion theory. Evaluate how different architectures impact these bounds.
2. Implement a systematic pruning algorithm based on the derived bounds, and compare its performance against existing empirical methods on benchmark datasets like CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
3. Investigate quantization strategies informed by the rate-distortion framework, measuring the accuracy and distortion trade-offs across various bit-width configurations.
4. Evaluate the compressibility of neural networks using mutual information measures and information bottleneck principles, validating predictions through simulation and empirical tests.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the difficulty in deriving precise theoretical bounds for complex architectures, the challenge of translating theoretical insights into practical algorithms, and the possibility that empirical results may not align perfectly with theoretical predictions. Additionally, the focus on theoretical rigor may limit the scope of practical applicability in specific hardware contexts.
